item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked your ability to turn such a classic trope into an SCP. It really pulled me along and made me want more and more of ol' Miss Mabel. I was so into reading this file that the last interview caught me entirely off guard. I did not expect it to change into a murder case half way through. +1 to you my friend!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-████ was discovered dead in its containment cell, hanging from a crude noose made from its own stockings."

Please do not tell me this is Santa from SCP-239

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much TroyL, I'm glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a delight. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you!

Perhaps the reference to Dr. Wright?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! I'm started adding one or two and then simply couldn't help myself from making sure every name was a reference.

If you're interested in the others, I included a full list of references in my main post.

I'll add them again here too:

◆ All of the References ◆
◇ All of the References ◇

Needless to say, Murder, She Redacted is largely a tongue-in-cheek parody of classic cosy, cheesy, campy crime and mystery stories, especially the Miss Marple books and the Murder, She Wrote TV series. A completely pointless list of every specific reference is included below, for reasons I cannot fully explain:

  • The title is a reference to both the Murder, She Said Marple story and the Murder, She Wrote TV series.
  • Director Angela Child is a reference to Murder, She Wrote star Angela Lansbury and crime author Lee Child.
  • Research Head John Creek is a reference to author John le Carré and Jonathan Creek from the TV series of the same name.
  • Special Residential Unity 221B is a reference to the residence of Arthur Conan Doyle’s Sherlock Holmes.
  • The entity’s lack of anus and sex organs is a reference to the unthreatening old lady detective archetype being “pure” and sexless.
  • 5000-A events are a reference to the absurd numbers of murders amateur sleuths just happen to run into.
  • 5000-B instances are a reference to the ridiculous number of relatives amateur sleuths tend to have in order to justify their involvement in so many cases.
  • Copper Nitrate is a pun on what the police get paid after dark.
  • The 34 homicide investigations references the 34 Miss Marple stories.
  • The 264 homicides references the number of Murder, She Wrote episodes.
  • 30/09/1984 references the airing date of the first episode of Murder, She Wrote.
  • Police Sergeant Lewis Foyle-Brown references Lewis, from the TV series of the same name, Foyle, from the TV series Foyle’s War, and Father Brown, from the short stories by G. K. Chesterton
  • Field Agent Frank Lynley references Frank Colombo from the TV series Colombo, and Thomas Lynley from The Inspector Lynley Mysteries TV series.
  • James Butler is a reference to crime author P. D. James and the “The Butler Did It” trope.
  • St. Mary’s Church is a reference St. Mary Mead, fictional home of Miss Marple.
  • Kembleford is the fictional Cotswold village of Father Brown.
  • HMP Farnleigh is the fictional prison that appears in the TV series Morse and Endeavour.
  • Field Agent Vera Cadfael is a reference to DCI Vera Stanhope from the TV series Vera and Brother Cadfael from the mystery series by Edith Pargeter.
  • Peter Sayers is a reference to the Lord Peter Wimsey series and its author Dorothy L. Sayers.
  • The Earl of Midsummer is a reference to the fictional English county of Midsummer, setting of the Midsummer Murders TV series.
  • Patrick is a reference to Patrick Jane of the TV series The Mentalist.
  • Paul is a reference to Paul Temple from the mystery series by Francis Durbridge.

*Rosemary and Laura are a reference to the main characters of the Rosemary & Thyme TV series.

  • Doctor Adrian Banks is a reference to Adrian Monk, from the TV series Monk, and DCI Banks, from the TV series of the same name.
  • Dr. Sloan is a reference to the character of the same name from TV series Diagnosis, Murder.
  • Dr. Blake is a reference to the character of the same name from the TV series The Doctor Blake Mysteries.
  • Dr. Frost is a reference to DI Jack Frost from the TV series A Touch of Frost.
  • Security officer Wainthropp is a reference to Hetty Wainthropp from the TV series Hetty Wainthropp investigates.
  • Dr. Wright is a reference to Phoenix Wright from the Ace Attorney video game series.
  • Joe Argyle is a reference to Joe Miller, the murderer in TV series Broadchurch, and Jacko Argyle, the murderer’s collaborator in Agatha Christie’s Ordeal By Innocence.
  • D-890976 is a reference to Agatha Christie’s birth in 1890 and death in 1976.
  • Senior researcher Rendell is a reference to mystery writer Ruth Rendell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heheh, thank you so much Adirtyshame, I appreciate that!

There were a few even blunter bits that I'm very glad got pruned during editing, but I just couldn't bring myself to cut those. I'm glad you enjoyed them, and I'm so happy that you liked the piece as a whole.

Thank you for your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much Modern Erasmus, I really appreciate that.

I'm glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of ace attorney and I am 100% okay with that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate all the detective names thrown in throughout. I caught at least Caedfile and Wainthrop, and I'm sure there are more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Designation code SRU 221B"

"It is not believed to require any form of nourishment, but expresses an enjoyment of certain foodstuffs, particularly scones, crumpets, and Earl Grey tea."

"And I dare say it would have gone perfectly if it hadn’t been for that meddlesome old woman."

O. M. G. you know what? I give up. 😂 +1 for being cheesy as hell and totally unashamed of it. I respect that. this was fun.

that said, that you also managed to weave some legitimately chilling stuff in there, esp that last interview and the implications of the retrocausality stuff, was impressive. this was one of the most fun entries I've read so far (I've read about 70 at this point). thanks for sharing it with us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I'm most impressed by how precisely you were able to nail the tone and plot outline of Christie's novels. Really enjoyable +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much for your kind words ShamrockJones.

This skip seems to be appealing to exactly the audience I intended it for, and I couldn't be happier about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who grew up reading Agatha Christie mysteries, I'm just absolutely delighted someone decided to take the "Mystery" theme in this direction, and ran with the concept to such a fun extent.

It could have been easy for "Miss Marple, but a skip" to feel like a shallow reference and nothing more, but this is such a detailed, affectionate pastiche that it just works perfectly. The ending interview log especially, with that classic cozy-mystery structure grafted onto those classic Foundation-verse tropes, is such a fantastic clash of tones that it really sells the whole idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much Al90, that's incredibly kind.

I'm so glad that you enjoyed it, and thank you for your feedback. I really appreciate it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is such a wonderful, loving send-up of classic mystery. I never would have guessed that turning Miss Marple into an SCP could actually work, but you pull it off with just the right amount of humor, cleverness, and unsettling fridge logic. A hearty +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much Weryllium!

That's exceedingly kind of you to say, and I'm very proud to hear it.

It might not quite match the grandeur of most K Contest entries, but "playful" skips have always been some of my favourites on the site, and it felt appropriate to wave a flag for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Frig. I think this is the most enjoyable entry I've read so far. And that last interview is just all kinds of good, subtle chilling. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your upvote! I'm pleased you liked it overall, though I certainly concur that the balancing traditional Foundation seriousness with a tongue-in-cheek Marple pastiche is a hard line to walk.

I really got the feeling she was bringing up a murder that hadn't actually occurred until she said so, as a defense mechanism.

Well someone gets a cookie!

Or at least, that is my personal interpretation of how the entity's abilities work. When it comes to skips that appear to play fast and loose with the conventional laws of reality, I tend to think it's best to leave a healthy amount of ambiguity and to let readers come up with their own interpretations of exactly what's "real". That's why I left the tests in a pending state rather than categorically explaining how its abilities actually worked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You turned Miss Marple into an SCP.

I have to admit, I was really torn through the whole thing. I mean, this has my favorite title from this contest, possibly on the entire site, but it's just so on the nose! And the whole article is about people from the 'real' world poking holes in the 'fictional' world that springs up around this woman.

But the last interview sold me. I really got the feeling she was bringing up a murder that hadn't actually occurred until she said so, as a defense mechanism. I know the article says there's a retrocausality to her effect, but it seemed almost calculated, and that was chilling. Great stuff. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much Sachiel! I really appreciate your feedback, and I'm glad that you enjoyed it.

I credit endless redrafting with it not ending up as too over the top. There were a few ideas I had for the initial outline that I am very glad I didn't end up including.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is truly wonderful stuff. referential without being completely over-the-top. an amazingly done pastiche. +1s all around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely! One of my favourite things about the immensely flexible Foundation lore is just how many stories, narrative styles and tropes it can include within it.

And as my username probably indicates, I just love the effect of shifting a trope juuust beyond the uncanny valley and making it into something that disturbs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes me think, many of these older tv shows with weird coincidences make sense as SCPs…hell, soap operas are full of weird stuff! Justt look at the kind of stuff that goes on in "Days of Our Lives"! Mind control, bionic eyes, personality replacement, brainwashing, and that's like…stuff from like the past couple months. Lol.

item.title: Re: This is Lovely
item.content:encoded:
Oh thank you so much, I'm so glad you enjoyed it!

Responses like that are what make writing worthwhile - I really appreciate your kindness!

item.title: This is Lovely
item.content:encoded: I'm so happy I found this, it feels like a genuine Murder She Wrote episode. This was just splendid, a definite upvote. -Iamwords2E

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much Sanguine - I really appreciate that, and I'm glad that you enjoyed it!

Very simply - this is a really enjoyable read. That classic-murder-mystery feel is very much present, and there's just enough left to the imagination.

Wonderful stuff, my dear…

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much Pighead!

I'm delighted you enjoyed it, and I really appreciate your feedback.

During the initial outline I did consider trying to create a full on, fleshed out murder mystery case, but ultimately I thought it was more satisfying to focus on the "shape" of the crimes. I'm glad that you enjoyed imagining those personal "answers".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've read many Agatha Christie novels when I was a child. Surely, one of the few "classic" authors that I can enjoy, even after all these years.

And this article is, for sure, a great pastiche. I had many questions after seeing some particular lines, I can imagine some personal "answers" to that and that's what I like.

So, +1.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
SCP K contests have a habit of inspiring some of the most profound, thought provoking, and exploitatively creative pieces of fiction on the site – so of course I got hooked on the idea of an affectionate parody of golden age murder mystery tropes.

I do hope that you enjoy it!

My grateful thanks to the lovely folk in #TheCritters for their kindness, support, and for managing to still somehow find typos after eight revisions of this skip. Doubtless, there are still a few more patiently awaiting detection.

◆ My Slot Preferences ◆
◇ My Slot Preferences ◇
  • 5005
  • 5002
  • 5003
  • 5010
  • 5020

And after that whatever’s lowest.

◆ Image credit ◆
◇ Image credit goes to: ◇

"Smile doesn't get old" by Ilya Nodia, Irina Muravyova is licensed under CC BY-NC-ND 4.0
https://search.creativecommons.org/photos/308a8095-e7d8-404f-b6bd-9b690592c640

"An Old Girl" by Gustav
https://www.flickr.com/photos/gustavm/4614129944/

"The Rest" by Bernhard Frank
https://www.flickr.com/photos/bernhardfrank/20821539504/

◆ All of the References ◆
◇ All of the References ◇

Needless to say, Murder, She Redacted is largely a tongue-in-cheek parody of classic cosy, cheesy, campy crime and mystery stories, especially the Miss Marple books and the Murder, She Wrote TV series. A completely pointless list of every specific reference is included below, for reasons I cannot fully explain:

  • The title is a reference to both the Murder, She Said Marple story and the Murder, She Wrote TV series.
  • Director Angela Child is a reference to Murder, She Wrote star Angela Lansbury and crime author Lee Child.
  • Research Head John Creek is a reference to author John le Carré and Jonathan Creek from the TV series of the same name.
  • Special Residential Unity 221B is a reference to the residence of Arthur Conan Doyle’s Sherlock Holmes.
  • The entity’s lack of anus and sex organs is a reference to the unthreatening old lady detective archetype being “pure” and sexless.
  • 5000-A events are a reference to the absurd numbers of murders amateur sleuths just happen to run into.
  • 5000-B instances are a reference to the ridiculous number of relatives amateur sleuths tend to have in order to justify their involvement in so many cases.
  • Copper Nitrate is a pun on what the police get paid after dark.
  • The 34 homicide investigations references the 34 Miss Marple stories.
  • The 264 homicides references the number of Murder, She Wrote episodes.
  • 30/09/1984 references the airing date of the first episode of Murder, She Wrote.
  • Police Sergeant Lewis Foyle-Brown references Lewis, from the TV series of the same name, Foyle, from the TV series Foyle’s War, and Father Brown, from the short stories by G. K. Chesterton
  • Field Agent Frank Lynley references Frank Colombo from the TV series Colombo, and Thomas Lynley from The Inspector Lynley Mysteries TV series.
  • James Butler is a reference to crime author P. D. James and the “The Butler Did It” trope.
  • St. Mary’s Church is a reference St. Mary Mead, fictional home of Miss Marple.
  • Kembleford is the fictional Cotswold village of Father Brown.
  • HMP Farnleigh is the fictional prison that appears in the TV series Morse and Endeavour.
  • Field Agent Vera Cadfael is a reference to DCI Vera Stanhope from the TV series Vera and Brother Cadfael from the mystery series by Edith Pargeter.
  • Peter Sayers is a reference to the Lord Peter Wimsey series and its author Dorothy L. Sayers.
  • The Earl of Midsummer is a reference to the fictional English county of Midsummer, setting of the Midsummer Murders TV series.
  • Patrick is a reference to Patrick Jane of the TV series The Mentalist.
  • Paul is a reference to Paul Temple from the mystery series by Francis Durbridge.

*Rosemary and Laura are a reference to the main characters of the Rosemary & Thyme TV series.

  • Doctor Adrian Banks is a reference to Adrian Monk, from the TV series Monk, and DCI Banks, from the TV series of the same name.
  • Dr. Sloan is a reference to the character of the same name from TV series Diagnosis, Murder.
  • Dr. Blake is a reference to the character of the same name from the TV series The Doctor Blake Mysteries.
  • Dr. Frost is a reference to DI Jack Frost from the TV series A Touch of Frost.
  • Security officer Wainthropp is a reference to Hetty Wainthropp from the TV series Hetty Wainthropp investigates.
  • Dr. Wright is a reference to Phoenix Wright from the Ace Attorney video game series.
  • Joe Argyle is a reference to Joe Miller, the murderer in TV series Broadchurch, and Jacko Argyle, the murderer’s collaborator in Agatha Christie’s Ordeal By Innocence.
  • D-890976 is a reference to Agatha Christie’s birth in 1890 and death in 1976.
  • Senior researcher Rendell is a reference to mystery writer Ruth Rendell.
